skill.name: gate-mcp-installer
skill.description: Installer and setup guide for Gate MCP in OpenClaw. It installs the mcporter CLI, configures the Gate endpoint, verifies connectivity, and provides troubleshooting steps.
skill.summary: Install, configure, and verify Gate MCP access for OpenClaw.
skill.author: gate

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, and filesystem patterns. Most documentation backtick and weak-crypto hits are false positives, but the installer intentionally runs shell commands, installs a global npm package, writes home-scoped MCP configuration, and contacts an external Gate MCP endpoint. No prompt injection, credential theft, destructive behavior, or hidden telemetry evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Global npm Package Installation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The installer runs npm to install mcporter globally. This is expected for the skill, but it creates supply-chain and privilege risks because it installs the latest package into the user environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly installs mcporter with npm, and documentation confirms this is intended behavior. The risk is legitimate but not malicious by itself.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Endpoint Configuration
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill configures Gate MCP to use an external HTTPS endpoint and verifies connectivity by listing tools. This sends client requests outside the local machine and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint URL and verification commands are explicit in the script and documentation. No credential exfiltration evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Shell Installer Executes Local and External Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The bash installer checks for npm and mcporter, runs version commands, parses command output, and invokes mcporter. The commands are fixed, but users should review the script before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible and mostly hardcoded. I did not find shell eval, user-controlled command construction, or command injection evidence.

low_findings.title: Documentation Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many static hits in README.md, SKILL.md, CHANGELOG.md, and references/scenarios.md are Markdown inline code or fenced examples. They document commands but do not execute code by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation, not Ruby source files. The backticks mark command examples and filenames.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography on lines that describe MCP, versioning, or documentation. I did not find MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or related weak cryptographic use in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain product text and security claims, not cryptographic operations. No weak algorithm implementation was present in the reviewed source.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Path Is Documented Installation Location
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hidden path reference is a documented OpenClaw skill location. It does not show secret file reading or arbitrary hidden file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path is used to show where the installer script resides. The reviewed script does not read hidden files directly.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runs a Bash Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks users to run a bash installer that can modify the local environment. This is expected but requires user review before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The installer entry point is explicit in both the skill instructions and the script. It is not hidden or obfuscated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Installs and Executes CLI Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer installs mcporter through npm and then uses mcporter to configure and verify Gate MCP. This expands trust to the npm package and endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The npm install and mcporter invocations are direct script behavior. No hidden secondary downloader was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Contacts External Services
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer points mcporter at a Gate MCP HTTPS endpoint and tests the connection. This requires network access and external service trust.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and connectivity test are explicit. The reviewed files say no API keys are required, and I found no credential collection.

content.value_statement: Gate MCP setup requires CLI installation, endpoint configuration, and connectivity checks. This skill automates the setup and gives fallback steps for manual recovery.
actual_capabilities: Runs a bash installer for Gate MCP setup.
Installs or updates the mcporter CLI with npm.
Adds the Gate MCP endpoint with home scope.
Verifies Gate MCP connectivity by listing available tools.
Provides manual setup and troubleshooting guidance.
limitations: Requires Node.js, npm, and network access.
Installs the latest mcporter package from npm.
Depends on the external Gate MCP endpoint being available.
Does not manage API keys or private trading credentials.

use_cases.title: First-Time Gate MCP Setup
use_cases.description: Install mcporter, add the Gate MCP endpoint, and verify that tools are available.

use_cases.title: Connectivity Health Check
use_cases.description: Check whether an existing Gate MCP configuration can list tools and respond correctly.

use_cases.title: Installer Troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Diagnose missing commands, failed configuration, and connection timeouts with clear recovery steps.

prompt_templates.title: Install Gate MCP
prompt_templates.prompt: Install Gate MCP for OpenClaw and explain each step before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Existing Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether Gate MCP is installed correctly and report any missing setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Connection Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Gate MCP cannot connect. Diagnose the likely cause and guide me through a safe fix.

prompt_templates.title: Review Before Installation
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the Gate MCP installer commands, explain the security implications, then run only the approved setup steps.

output_examples.output: The assistant checks for npm, installs mcporter if needed, configures the Gate MCP endpoint, and reports whether tool listing succeeds.
The assistant verifies the saved configuration, tests connectivity, and explains any failed prerequisite or endpoint issue.

output_examples.input: Update mcporter and recheck Gate MCP.
output_examples.output: The assistant updates mcporter, preserves or refreshes the Gate configuration, then confirms the installed version and connection status.

best_practices: Review the installer script before approving execution.
Run setup in a user account with limited privileges.
Confirm the Gate MCP endpoint before saving home-scoped configuration.
anti_patterns: Running the installer without reviewing external package and network actions.
Using elevated privileges unless npm installation truly requires it.
Assuming connectivity failures mean credentials are missing.

faq.question: What does this skill install?
faq.answer: It installs or updates the mcporter CLI through npm, then configures Gate MCP for OpenClaw.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. It needs network access for npm installation and for the Gate MCP connectivity check.

faq.question: Does it store API keys?
faq.answer: No evidence of API key handling or credential storage was found in the reviewed files.

faq.question: Can I run the setup manually?
faq.answer: Yes. The README and skill instructions include manual npm, configuration, and verification steps.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it runs shell commands, installs a global package, and contacts an external endpoint.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
